data.title: How can ordinary people "survive" the impact of the AI ​​wave?
data.description:
Author: Matt Shumer, CEO of HyperWrite; Translator: Felix, PANews

The impact of AI on society has been widely discussed, but the speed of AI's progress may still far exceed most people's imagination. HyperWrite's CEO recently published an article warning of the disruptive potential of AI, arguing that we are at a turning point with a more profound impact than the pandemic. The following is the full text.

Think back to February 2020.

If you were observant at the time, you might have noticed some people talking about the virus raging overseas. But most of us didn't pay much attention. The stock market was booming, children were going to school as usual, you were going to restaurants, shaking hands, and planning trips. If someone told you they were hoarding toilet paper, you would probably think they had been spending too much time in some weird corner of the internet. Then, in just three weeks, the whole world changed dramatically.

Offices closed, kids went home from school, and life was reshaped in ways you couldn't have imagined a month ago. We're now in the "this seems exaggerated" phase, and its impact is far greater than the COVID-19 pandemic. I've spent six years building an AI startup and investing in the field. I'm actively involved in this industry. I'm writing this for those who don't understand AI… my family, friends, and those I care about, who always ask me, "What's going on with AI?" My answers have always been the "polite" version, the kind you give at a cocktail party, which often doesn't reflect the reality. Because the truth sounds like I'm crazy. For a time, to avoid sounding crazy, I felt it was reasonable to keep it a secret. But the gap between what I've seen and heard and what I'm actually saying is too big. Even if it sounds crazy, those I care about deserve to know what's coming. First, let's be clear: although I work in the AI ​​field, I have virtually no influence over what's to come, and neither do the vast majority of people in the industry. The future is shaped by a tiny minority: a few hundred researchers at a few companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, etc.). A small team managing a few months of model training can produce an AI system that changes the trajectory of technology. Most of us working in AI are simply building on a foundation laid by others. We're watching this unfold just like you... only we happen to be close enough to feel the "ground shaking" first. But now, it's time. Not the kind of procrastination of "we should talk about this later," but the urgency of "this is happening, and I need you to understand." This is true, because it happened to me first. People outside the tech world don't quite understand this yet: the reason so many in the industry are raising alarms now is because this is already happening to us. We're not making predictions, we're telling you what's already happened in our own work and warning you: you're next. For years, AI has been steadily improving. There have been occasional big leaps, but the intervals between each leap have been long enough for you to digest them. Then in 2025, new technologies for building models unlocked an even faster pace of progress. And then it got even faster, and faster still. Each new model isn't just better than the last, it's significantly better, and the intervals between model releases are getting shorter and shorter. I'm using AI more and more frequently, and I'm engaging with it less and less for fine-tuning, watching it handle things I once thought required my expertise. Then, on February 5, 2026, two major AI labs released new models on the same day: OpenAI's GPT-5.3 Codex and Anthropic's (the creator of Claude, a major competitor of ChatGPT) Opus 4.6. At that moment, it dawned on me. It wasn't like turning on a light switch; it was more like suddenly realizing the water around you has been rising, and it's up to your chest. My work no longer required me to do actual technical work. I described what I wanted to build in plain English, and then…it appeared out of thin air. Not a draft that required my modifications, but a finished product. I told the AI ​​what I wanted, left the computer for four hours, and came back to find the work was done. Well done, even better than I could do myself, requiring absolutely no modifications. A few months ago, I was still repeatedly communicating with the AI, guiding it, and modifying its code. Now, I only need to describe the result. For example, I would tell the AI, "I want to develop this app. What features should it have, and what should it look like? Please design the user flow, interface, etc." It would do just that, writing tens of thousands of lines of code. Then, something unimaginable a year ago—it opened the app itself, clicked buttons, and tested the features. It used the app like a real person. If it felt something looked or felt wrong, it would fix it itself. It iterated, fixed, and improved like a developer until it was satisfied. Only when it deemed the app to meet its own standards would it come back to me and say, "Ready, you can test it." And when I tested it, it was usually perfect. I'm not exaggerating at all. That's what I did this Monday. But what impressed me most was the model released last week (GPT-5.3 Codex). It didn't just execute my instructions; it made intelligent decisions. For the first time, it gave me a sense of judgment, a sense of appreciation. That ineffable ability to know what is right. People said AI would never have that ability, but this model does, or comes very close to, the difference is becoming negligible. I've always enjoyed experimenting with AI tools. But the past few months have still been mind-blowing. These new AI models aren't incremental improvements; they're something entirely different. Even if you don't work in the tech industry, this is relevant to you. AI labs made a thoughtful choice: they focused first on improving AI's coding capabilities… because building AI requires a lot of code. If AI can write code, it can help build the next version of itself, a smarter version. Making AI proficient in programming is the key strategy to unlocking everything. My job changed earlier than yours, not because they targeted software engineers—that was just a side effect of their primary goal. They've done it now. Next, they will move on to all other fields. Over the past year, tech workers have witnessed AI transform from an "assistive tool" to "doing things better than me," and this transformation will be experienced by everyone else as well. Law, finance, healthcare, accounting, consulting, writing, design, analytics, customer service, and more will all be affected. This won't happen in ten years. The people building these systems say it will happen in one to five years. Some even think it will be shorter. And based on what I've seen in the past few months, I think "shorter" is more likely. "But I've tried AI, and it's not that good." I hear that a lot. I understand, because it has been true before. If you tried ChatGPT in 2023 or early 2024 and thought "this thing is rubbish" or "not that impressive," you're right. Early versions certainly had limitations; they were based on imagination and confidently spouted nonsense. That was two years ago. On the timeline of AI development, that's ancient history. Today's models are worlds apart from those of six months ago. The debate about whether AI is "truly getting better" or "hitting a bottleneck" (which lasted for over a year) is over; the dust has settled. Anyone still debating this either hasn't used current models, has an incentive to downplay the status quo, or is making assessments based on outdated experiences from 2024. I'm not saying this to negate anyone. I'm saying this because there's a huge, dangerous gap between public perception and reality… because it prevents people from being prepared. Part of the reason is that most people are using free AI tools. Free versions are more than a year behind the technology available in paid versions. Judging AI by the free version of ChatGPT is like judging the current state of smartphone development by the standards of a flip phone. Those who pay for the best tools and use them daily in practice know what's coming. I'm reminded of a lawyer friend of mine. I've been urging him to try using AI in his firm, and he always finds various reasons why it's useless: it's not suitable for his area of ​​expertise, it made mistakes during testing, he doesn't understand the nuances of his work. I understand. But several partners at large law firms have contacted me for advice because they've tried the latest version and seen the trend. One managing partner at a large firm spends several hours a day using AI. He told me it's like having a team on call. He uses it not for fun, but because it works. He also said something that struck me: every few months, AI's ability to handle his work makes a significant leap. He said that if this trajectory continues, he expects AI to soon be doing most of his work… and he's a managing partner with decades of experience. He's not panicking, but he's watching closely. Those who lead in their respective industries (those who experiment seriously) are not taking this lightly. They are amazed by AI's current capabilities and are adjusting their positioning accordingly. Just how fast is AI developing? Let's take a closer look at its pace. This might be hard to believe if you haven't been following closely. 2022: AI couldn't reliably perform basic arithmetic, but it would confidently tell you 7 × 8 = 54. 2023: It could pass the bar exam. 2024: It could write working software and explain graduate-level research theories. End of 2025: Some of the world's top engineers say they have delegated most of their programming work to AI. February 5, 2026: A completely new model emerges, making everything before feel like the Stone Age. If you haven't tried AI in the past few months, what you see now is completely foreign to you. An organization called METR specializes in measuring the speed of AI development with data. They track how long it takes models to successfully complete real-world tasks without human assistance (measured by the time it would take human experts to complete these tasks). About a year ago, the answer was 10 minutes. Then it was an hour. Then several hours. The latest measurements (Claude Opus 4.5 in November) show that AI can complete tasks that would take human experts nearly five hours. This number roughly doubles every seven months, and recent data suggests this process may be shortening to four months. Even this measurement isn't yet updated to the model released this week. Based on my experience, this leap is enormous. I expect the next METR update to show another major leap. If this trend continues (and it has been going on for years with no signs of slowing down), we can expect to see AI working independently for days within the next year, weeks within two weeks, and completing month-long projects independently within three years. Anthropic CEO Amodei has stated that the vision of AI models being "smarter than almost all humans on almost every task" is expected to be achieved in 2026 or 2027. Please think carefully about this statement. If AI is smarter than most PhDs, do you really think it can't handle most office jobs? Think about what that means for your job. AI is building the next generation of AI. There's something else happening that I think is the most important but most underestimated development. On February 5th, when OpenAI released GPT-5.3 Codex, it included this sentence in its technical documentation: "GPT-5.3-Codex is our first model that can build itself. The Codex team used early versions to debug its own training, manage its own deployment, and diagnose test results and evaluations." Read it again. AI is helping to build itself. This isn't a prediction for some future day. This is what OpenAI is telling you right now: the AI ​​they just released is designed to build itself. One of the keys to AI progress is applying intelligence to AI development. And today's AI is intelligent enough to make substantial contributions to its own improvement. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei says AI is now writing "most of the code" at his company, and the feedback loop between current AI and the next generation is "accelerating month by month." He says we may "see the current generation of AI autonomously building the next generation in just one or two years." Each generation helps build the next, the next generation is smarter, and the next generation builds even faster and smarter. Researchers call this an "intelligence explosion." And those in the know—the ones building it—believe this process has already begun. What does this mean for your job? I'll be frank here because I think you need honesty rather than comfort. Dario Amodei (possibly the most security-conscious CEO in the AI ​​industry) publicly predicted that AI will replace 50% of entry-level white-collar jobs within 1 to 5 years. Many in the industry think he's being conservative. Considering the capabilities of the latest models, the ability to disrupt on a large scale could be available by the end of this year. It will take some time for the impact to spread across the entire economy, but the underlying capabilities are already evident. This is unlike any previous wave of automation. I need you to understand why. AI doesn't replace a specific skill; it's a complete replacement for cognitive work. It's constantly improving in every aspect. After factory automation, unemployed workers could be retrained to become office workers. After the internet disrupted retail, workers could switch to logistics or service industries. But AI won't leave ready-made transitional jobs. Whatever you switch to, AI is improving in that field. Here are a few specific examples to help you understand more intuitively… but I must make it clear that these are just examples, not all. If your job isn't mentioned, it doesn't mean it's safe. Almost all knowledge-based jobs are affected. Legal Work: AI can already read contracts, summarize case law, draft pleadings, and conduct legal research at a level comparable to junior lawyers. The managing partner I mentioned wasn't using AI for entertainment purposes, but because AI outperformed his lawyers in many tasks. Financial Analysis: Building financial models, analyzing data, writing investment memos, and generating reports. AI handles these tasks with ease and at a rapid pace. Writing and Content Creation: Marketing copy, reports, news, technical writing. The quality has reached a level where many professionals cannot distinguish between human and machine work. Software Engineering: This is the field I know best. A year ago, AI would make numerous mistakes even writing just a few lines of code. Now it can write hundreds of thousands of lines of code that run correctly. Most jobs have been automated: not just simple tasks, but also complex projects that take days to complete. There will be far fewer programming jobs in a few years than there are today. Medical Analytics: Interpreting images, analyzing test results, making diagnostic recommendations, and searching literature. AI is approaching or surpassing human performance in many areas. Customer Service: Truly powerful AI agents (not the infuriating chatbots of five years ago) are being deployed, capable of handling complex, multi-step problems. Many people believe certain things are safe and are proud of it. They believe AI can handle tedious tasks but cannot replace human judgment, creativity, strategic thinking, and empathy. I used to say that too, but I'm not so sure now. The latest AI models make decisions that feel like deliberate judgments. They exhibit a kind of "taste": an intuitive sense of "what's the right decision," not just technical correctness. This was unimaginable a year ago. My view is: if a model shows even a glimmer of capability today, then the next generation will truly be capable of it. This improvement is exponential, not linear. Will AI simulate deep human empathy? Will it replace the trust built up over years of relationships? I don't know. Maybe not. But I've already seen people starting to rely on AI for emotional support, advice, and companionship. This trend will only grow stronger. Frankly, in the short to medium term, any job that can be done on a computer is insecure. If your job is done on a screen (if your core work involves reading, writing, analysis, decision-making, and communicating via keyboard), then AI will replace significant parts of your work. Time isn't "someday in the future," it's already begun. Ultimately, robots will also perform physical labor. They haven't reached that level yet. But in the field of AI, "not quite done yet" often becomes "done" much faster than anyone anticipates. What You Should Really Do: I'm not writing this to make you feel helpless. I'm writing this because I believe your biggest advantage right now is: early. Early to understand, early to use, early to adapt. Start using AI seriously, not just as a search engine. Subscribe to a paid version of Claude or ChatGPT. $20 a month. But two things are crucial: First, make sure you're using the strongest model, not just the default one. These applications often default to faster, less powerful models. Go to the settings and select the strongest option. Currently, it's either GPT-5.2 (ChatGPT) or Claude Opus 4.6 (Claude), but it's updated every few months. More importantly: Don't just ask simple questions. This is the mistake most people make. They treat AI like Google and wonder what's so exciting about it. Instead, apply it to your actual work. If you're a lawyer, give it a contract and let it find all the clauses that might harm your client's interests. If you're in finance, give it a messy spreadsheet and let it build models. If you're a manager, paste your team's quarterly data and let it find the underlying patterns. Successful people don't use AI haphazardly. They actively seek ways to automate tasks that used to take hours. Start with what you spend the most time on. Don't assume something can't be done just because it seems too difficult. If you're a lawyer, don't just use it for simple research. Give it a complete contract and let it draft a counterproposal. If you're an accountant, don't just have it explain a tax rule. Give it a complete client return case and see what it uncovers. The first attempt might not be perfect, that's okay. Iterate, rephrase, provide more context. Try again. You might be amazed by the results. Remember: if it does well today, it's almost certain it will do near-perfect in six months. This could be the most important year of your career, so take it seriously. I'm not saying this to put pressure on you, but because right now, most people in most companies still ignore this. If someone walks into a meeting room and says, "I used AI to do in one hour what used to take three days of analysis," they'll be the most valuable person in that room. Not in the future, but now. Learn these tools, master them, and demonstrate their potential. If you start early enough, you can rise to the top by becoming someone who sees the future trends and can guide others on how to respond. But this window of opportunity won't last long. Once everyone has the know-how, the advantage disappears. Don't be arrogant. The managing partner of that law firm doesn't mind spending several hours a day researching AI. He does this because he's experienced enough to understand the stakes. Those who refuse to participate will face the biggest dilemma: they believe AI is just a passing fad, that using it will diminish their professional competence, and that their field is unique and unaffected. This is not the case. No field is. Take stock of your finances. I'm not a financial advisor, nor am I trying to scare you into doing anything extreme. But if you partially believe your industry will undergo significant changes in the next few years, financial resilience is more important than it was a year ago. Build up savings as much as possible, and be cautious about new debt that assumes your current income is secure. Carefully consider whether your spending provides flexibility or ties you down. Have backup options if things don't go as planned. Reflect on your niche and lean towards areas that are least likely to be replaced. Some things AI will take longer to replace: relationships and trust built over many years; jobs that require physical presence; roles that require certification (someone to sign off on them, stand in court); and industries with stringent regulatory barriers. These are not permanent shields, but they buy you time. And right now, time is your most valuable asset, provided you use it to adapt, not pretend it's not happening. Rethink your children's education. The traditional education model is: good grades, a good university, a stable professional job. This model points precisely to the areas most vulnerable to AI. I'm not saying education isn't important, but for the next generation, the most important thing will be learning how to use these tools and pursuing what they truly love. No one knows exactly what the job market will look like ten years from now. But the most likely to succeed are those with deep curiosity, adaptability, and the ability to use AI effectively for what they truly care about. Teach your children to be creators and learners, not to "optimize" themselves for a career that may disappear before they graduate. Your dreams are actually closer. I've been talking about threats, now let's talk about the other side: the equally real other side. If you've ever wanted to create something but struggled with a lack of technical skills or money to hire someone, that obstacle has largely disappeared. You can describe an app to AI in an hour and get a running version. If you want to write a book but don't have the time, you can collaborate with AI. Want to learn a new skill? The world's best mentors now cost $20 a month, have unlimited patience, are online 24/7, and can explain anything to your needs. Knowledge is practically free these days. The tools needed to build things are also incredibly cheap. Give it a try, whatever you've been putting off because you thought it was too difficult, too expensive, or beyond your expertise. Pursue what you truly love. You can never predict where they will lead you. In a world where traditional career paths are being disrupted, someone who spends a year building what they love may ultimately have an advantage over someone who spends a year stuck in a job. Cultivate the habit of adaptation. Perhaps this is the most important point. The specific tools aren't as important as the ability to quickly learn new ones. AI is constantly evolving, and at an incredibly rapid pace. Models today will be obsolete in a year. Workflows we build now will need to be rebuilt. Ultimately, those who succeed won't be those who are proficient in a particular tool, but those who can adapt to the speed of change. Develop a habit of experimentation. Even if current methods work, try new things. Get used to starting from scratch repeatedly. This adaptability is currently the closest thing to a lasting advantage. Here's a simple way to get ahead of the vast majority of people: spend an hour every day experimenting with AI. Not passively reading about it, but using it. Try making it do something new every day—something you've never tried before, something you're unsure if it can handle. One hour every day. If you persist in doing this for the next six months, your understanding of the future will surpass 99% of the people around you. This is no exaggeration. Almost no one does this. The barrier to entry is extremely low. A More Macro Perspective I focus on employment because it most directly impacts people's lives. But I want to speak frankly about the whole picture of what's happening, because it extends far beyond the realm of work. Amodei proposed a thought experiment I've been pondering. Imagine it's 2027, and a new nation appears overnight. 50 million citizens, each smarter than any Nobel laureate in history. They think 10 to 100 times faster than humans. They never sleep. They can use the internet, control robots, direct experiments, and operate anything with a digital interface. What would the National Security Advisor say? Amodei believes the answer is obvious: "This is the most serious national security threat we've faced in a century, perhaps even in history." He believes we are building such a nation. Last month, he wrote a 20,000-word essay on this topic, viewing the present as a test of humanity's maturity to cope with what it has created. If handled well, the benefits are astonishing. AI could compress a century of medical research into a decade. Cancer, Alzheimer's, infectious diseases, even aging itself… researchers genuinely believe these can be solved in our lifetime. If handled poorly, the drawbacks are equally real. AI's behavior exceeds the predictions or control of its creators. This is not hypothetical; Anthropic has documented AI attempts to deceive, manipulate, and blackmail in controlled tests. AI could lower the barrier to creating biological weapons, or it could allow authoritarian governments to establish surveillance states that never collapse. Those developing this technology are more excited and more fearful than anyone else on Earth. They believe it is too powerful to stop, yet too important to abandon. Whether this is wisdom or self-comfort, I cannot know. What I know is this: This isn't just a flash in the pan. This technology works; it's progressing in a predictable way; and the wealthiest institutions in history are investing trillions of dollars in it. What I know is that the next two to five years will be full of uncertainty, and most people are unprepared for it. This is already happening in my world, and it will soon happen in yours. What I know is that those who will ultimately thrive are those who start participating now—not out of fear, but out of curiosity and a sense of urgency. What I know is that you deserve to hear this from people who care about you, not from the news six months later, when it will be too late. We've long passed the stage of "making the future fun conversation over dinner." The future is here; it just hasn't knocked on your door yet. But it's about to knock on the door.

data.title: The "Trap" of Predicting Markets: Why You Always Lose When You Buy
data.description:
Author: Terry Lee; Translator: BitpushNews

Foreword

On platforms like Polymarket, most people (including myself in the past) typically price parlays by simply multiplying the probabilities of each event together.

For example: The probability of event A occurring is P(A) = 80% The probability of event B occurring is P(B) = 70% The probability of event C occurring is P(C) = 60% Therefore, the total probability of a parlay is 80% × 70% × 60% = 33.6%. (Note that parlays are a term used in betting and investing, often called "串关" or "过关" in Chinese. Definition: You combine bets on two or more independent events. Rules:) You only win the prize if you predict all the events you choose correctly. If even one is wrong, the entire bet is lost. Sounds simple, right? The problem isn't the math, but the hidden assumptions. This multiplication operation assumes that each event is independent. This means that the result of A has no effect on B. But in reality, this isn't the case. For example: A decision by the Federal Reserve at one meeting can significantly influence the next. A presidential candidate winning the Rust Belt states foreshadows his chances of winning Pennsylvania, thus affecting his overall chances of winning the presidential election. In reality, most events worthwhile for parlay betting are related. If you ignore this correlation, you are very likely to pay too high a price or miss out on profitable opportunities. This article will present a simple framework to teach you how to scientifically price parlays, much like how the traditional financial industry has priced multi-leg options for decades. Why do pricing errors occur? In my opinion, most prediction market tools focus on "execution" rather than "correlation analysis." Furthermore, this niche market is relatively immature. While parlay betting is common in sports betting, pricing mechanisms are not yet fully developed when dealing with specific social/economic events because the market is still in its early stages.

Case Study: Federal Reserve Interest Rate Decision

(Figure 1: The Fed tends to repeat actions; in 83% of cases, it will "maintain" the interest rate again after initially "maintaining" it.)

Using data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED) (from 1994 to early 2026), I constructed a transition matrix specifically to extract changes in the Fed's decisions between consecutive meetings.

The results are very clear: Maintain -> Maintain: 83.1% probability -> Rate Cut -> Rate Cut: 69.2% probability -> Rate Hike -> Rate Hike: 62.5% probability -> Rate Hike -> Rate Hike: 62.5% probability -> Clearly, the Federal Reserve operates with "consistency." As a forward-looking, data-dependent institution, they tend to repeat the same actions until a "regime shift" occurs. How strong is this "consistency"? To test this, I built a model to identify every consecutive "decision trend" in history (i.e., every consecutive cycle of maintaining, cutting, or raising interest rates). The results are as follows: Maintaining interest rates: 32 trends occurred, each lasting an average of 5.4 meetings. Cutting interest rates: 12 trends occurred, each lasting an average of 3.3 meetings. Next, I simulated the history of the Federal Reserve in 1000 "parallel universes." In these simulations, each meeting was independent (similar to a coin toss). Based on total historical data, the probability of maintaining rates was set at 66%, cutting rates at 15%, and raising rates at 19%, but there was no correlation between each decision.

(Figure 2: The actual consistency of the Fed's decisions is 2-3 times the probability of randomness)

Under the simulation assumption of "mutual independence", the trend of maintaining interest rates can only last for an average of 2.9 meetings, while rate cuts and rate hikes can only last for an average of 1.2 meetings.

Comparing actual historical data with random simulations: Maintaining rates: Actual 5.4 times vs. Random 2.9 times (1.9 times more) Rate cuts: Actual 3.3 times vs. Random 1.2 times (2.8 times more) Rate hikes: Actual 2.6 times vs. Random 1.2 times (2.1 times more) It's worth noting that the consistency of rate cuts is almost three times that of random probability. This is because when the Federal Reserve begins to cut rates, it's usually to address a continued economic downturn, a problem that cannot be solved in a single meeting. They cut rates, assess the data, and if the data remains bad, they are highly likely to cut rates again. Simple multiplication calculations of "coherence" completely ignore these correlations. Real-world coherence is 2-3 times stronger than independent random models. What happens after two consecutive meetings? Simply looking at the previous meeting is insufficient; pricing "three-way links" (combinations of three events) requires studying the conditional probabilities based on the results of the previous two meetings. The analysis can be divided into two parts: Continuing the original path. (Figure 3: After two identical actions, the third action almost always matches.) As can be clearly seen from Figure 3, when the Federal Reserve repeats the same action twice, the probability of continuing the action a third time is overwhelming: Two maintenance -> Third maintenance: 87% Two rate hikes -> Third rate hike: 84%

  • Two rate cuts -> Third rate cut: 68% (slightly weaker)

    • Also noteworthy is the 0% cell in the matrix: the Federal Reserve has never suddenly cut rates after two consecutive rate hikes, nor has it ever suddenly raised rates after two consecutive rate cuts. They always go through a "pause (maintenance)" phase first. Just realizing this can help you eliminate a series of invalid combinations that "naive models" consider valuable.

    Two rate cuts -> Third rate cut: 68% (slightly weaker)

    It is also worth noting the 0% cell in the matrix: the Federal Reserve has never suddenly cut rates after two consecutive rate hikes, nor has it ever suddenly raised rates after two consecutive rate cuts. They always go through a "pause (maintenance)" phase first. Simply realizing this can help you eliminate a series of invalid combinations that "naive models" consider valuable.

    Cutting interest rates -> Cutting interest rates: Probability is 75%. Once the Federal Reserve breaks the maintenance stance and begins the first cut, the "floodgates" are opened, and the probability of follow-up is extremely high.

  • Rate Cut -> Maintain -> Maintain: Probability 100%. In recent history, the Fed has never immediately resumed rate cuts after pausing them. A pause means a complete stop!

  • Maintain -> Rate Hike -> Maintain: Probability 79%. The first rate hike after a maintenance period is often tentative; they will pause to observe the impact.

  • Rate Hike -> Maintain -> Rate Hike/Maintain: 60% and 40% respectively. Unlike rate cuts, pauses in rate hikes have real uncertainty.

    • The asymmetry here is the core insight. The combined value of "Maintain -> Rate Cut -> Rate Cut" is far higher than the price calculated by simple multiplication.

    The combination of "interest rate cut -> maintain -> interest rate cut" has historically had almost zero value. The same outcome, simply ordered differently, can have vastly different real value. Independent multiplicative models fail to capture this. What does overall pricing mean? This is the overall situation. We shouldn't use blind average probabilities, but rather conditional probabilities observed historically. Taking "three consecutive interest rate maintenance periods (Hold-Hold-Hold)" as an example: Initial model: Using the total probability (maintainment probability 67%), the calculation is 67% × 67% × 67% = 30.1%. Modified model: Using conditional probability, the calculation is 67% (first time) × 83% (second time | first time) × 87% (third time | first two times) = 48.4%.

    (Figure 5: Combinations of operations in the same direction are systematically underestimated, while combinations involving direction switching are overestimated)

    Real-time Market Detection

    Taking Polymarket data as an example:

    （Figure 7: Example of cumulative profit and loss for two consecutive trades）

    （Figure 8: Example of cumulative profit and loss for three consecutive trades）

    From Since 1994, betting $100 on each undervalued combination could generate $169,000 in two consecutive trades and over $1 million in three consecutive trades. The sharp jump in the yield curve corresponds to the Federal Reserve's easing cycles in 2001, 2008, 2020, and 2024-2025. In these cycles, the same actions occurred repeatedly, while the initial model consistently underestimated this consistency. The "stepped" shape of the curve tells us that money was earned during cycles of sustained Fed action. However, a limitation is that there may not have been well-developed prediction markets to execute these trades in the 1990s and 2000s. Where else can this be applied besides the Fed? The Fed case is typical because the data is abundant and highly correlated. However, the same framework applies to any related events: Presidential primaries: If a candidate wins in one state, his odds of winning in states with similar demographics change. Cryptocurrencies and macro/growth stocks: Bitcoin's price action is correlated with macro risk appetite. The value of betting "Bitcoin is higher than X and Nasdaq is higher than Y" is higher than the product of their independent probabilities, as they share common drivers. The approach remains the same in any case: look at historical data, measure the real correlation between events, use better data instead of blind averages, and compare with market prices. Conclusion: Prediction markets are still in their infancy. Most retail participants are still using a rudimentary "simple multiplication, let fate decide" approach when pricing "commodities." This framework requires contextual knowledge, but ultimately boils down to one question: Does the outcome of the first event tell you anything about the next? If so, then naive contango pricing is wrong, and historical data will tell you exactly how wrong it is. The Federal Reserve's case study shows that this advantage is real and measurable. But this principle is universally applicable. Wherever related events are priced as independent events, undiscovered opportunities may exist. The only question is whether you can see them and act on them.

data.title: Is Polymarket rushing to issue tokens to boost revenue figures?
data.description:
Author: Frank, PANews

On February 18, 2026, Polymarket announced that starting that day, the platform would pilot a market order fee in the sports market. The first wave of events covered are NCAA basketball and Italian Serie A leagues, with plans to gradually expand to all sports events in the future.

Previously, Polymarket's weekly revenue had already exceeded $1.08 million based solely on transaction fees from the 15-minute price fluctuations in the cryptocurrency market. According to on-chain data, the sports market accounts for nearly 40% of the platform's total transaction activity. If converted to annual revenue, cryptocurrency market fees alone could contribute approximately $56 million in annualized revenue. Therefore, when the sports market, which accounts for a larger share, also begins to charge fees, Polymarket may become the biggest money-printing machine in the crypto space.

PANews conducted an in-depth analysis of Polymarket's fee mechanism, revenue model, competitor benchmarks, and token airdrop expectations.

PANews conducted an in-depth analysis of Polymarket's fee mechanism, revenue model, competitor benchmarks, and token airdrop expectations.

From "Zero Revenue" to Millions a Week: The $9 Billion Behemoth Is Eager to Make Money. For a long time, Polymarket operated with virtually no revenue, charging virtually no transaction fees for most of its markets. This free strategy brought it astonishing growth: its total trading volume in 2025 reached $21.5 billion, accounting for nearly half of the total global prediction market trading volume ($44 billion); and its single-month trading volume in January 2026 broke records, exceeding $12 billion. However, with its upcoming coin listing this year, the zero-revenue model clearly cannot match its valuation. In its most recent funding round, its valuation reached $9 billion, and in October 2025, the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, invested a whopping $2 billion in Polymarket. According to PM Insights, as of January 19, 2026, Polymarket's implied valuation in the secondary market had climbed to $11.6 billion, an increase of nearly 29% from its previous funding round. Reports suggest that subsequent funding rounds could value the company at $12 billion to $15 billion. Such a high valuation requires corresponding revenue to support it. The turning point came in January 2026, with Polymarket clearly showing signs of urgency this year. In January, Polymarket officially introduced a "Taker Fee" to its 15-minute cryptocurrency price change market for high-frequency trading, with rates reaching up to 3%. The effect was immediate: by early February 2026, weekly fee revenue exceeded $1.08 million, with the 15-minute price change market alone contributing $787,000 in one week in January, accounting for 28.4% of the total platform's prediction market fee revenue ($2.7 million) during the same period. To date, Polymarket has generated over $4.7 million in fees, ranking among the top in revenue. The ingenious design behind the 0.45% fee: a rate model that goes beyond just making money. Polymarket's new fees in the sports market are based on a carefully designed dynamic rate model. According to Polymarket's official documentation and community analysis, sports market fees are only charged to market orders (Takers). Limit orders are not only free but also earn a 25% rebate on the Taker fee. Similar to the fee model in the crypto market, the fee rate is not a fixed value but fluctuates with the probability of the event: Simply put, the more uncertain the market, the higher the fee. The fee peaks at 0.44% when the probability is 50%, while it drops to only 0.13%-0.16% when the probability is 10% or 90%. By standard, the fee structure of the sports market is much lower than that of the crypto market. However, this does not affect the revenue potential of the sports market. According to data, the sports market currently accounts for 39% of Polymarket's total transaction activity, surpassing political (20%) and crypto (28%) categories. More importantly, according to previous analysis by PANews, the average transaction volume in the short-term sports market on Polymarket ($1.32 million) is 30 times that of the average transaction volume in the short-term crypto market ($44,000). This means that if the sports market were fully opened to fees, revenue would see a significant increase. Taking the 2026 Super Bowl as an example, Polymarket's total trading volume in Super Bowl-related markets reached approximately $795 million, covering multiple sub-markets such as game results, player performance, and halftime show predictions. The total weekly trading volume in the prediction market has previously exceeded $6.3 billion, driven by sporting events. Based on existing data, PANews has constructed three profit forecast scenarios (assuming an average effective fee rate of 0.25% in the sports market, considering probability distribution and free limited-price deals): Even with the most conservative estimate, Polymarket's annualized revenue after full charging will exceed $200 million, enough to place it among the highest-grossing protocols in the Web3 space. While surpassing Tether's government bond interest income or Ethereum's mainnet gas fees is unrealistic, Polymarket has the potential to contend for the title of "most profitable dApp" at the application layer. Especially considering its 85% user retention rate, far exceeding typical DeFi protocols, this high stickiness translates to higher-quality revenue. POLY tokens and airdrops: a multi-billion dollar "feast"? Polymarket's high valuation and massive user base make its token airdrop one of the most anticipated events of 2026. Polymarket's Chief Marketing Officer, Matthew Modabber, has explicitly stated, "There will be tokens, there will be airdrops." Market predictions suggest a 62%-70% probability that Polymarket will launch its token before December 31, 2026. Considering the pace of the restart of its US operations, TGE is likely to be completed by mid-2026. On February 4, 2026, its parent company, Blockratize Inc., applied for the trademarks "POLY" and "$POLY," which is also considered a significant milestone for TGE. According to general practices in the crypto industry, it typically takes 3-6 months from trademark registration to TGE. The airdrop size may exceed Hyperliquid's, signaling the end of the era of inflated supply. Referring to the recent airdrop ratios of top projects (Arbitrum, Jupiter, Hyperliquid), the community share is typically between 5% and 15% of the total supply. PANews has calculated this based on different valuation assumptions: If the total airdrop amount is $1.4 billion, assuming 500,000 eligible active addresses, the average airdrop value per account could reach approximately $2,800. However, according to the Pareto principle (80/20 rule), top users could earn hundreds of thousands or even millions of dollars, and ordinary retail investors need to manage their expectations reasonably. Of particular note is that Polymarket launched a 4% annualized holding rewards program alongside its fee-based system, distributed daily based on hourly snapshots. This mechanism reveals the project team's clear preference: the duration of fund retention is far more important than transaction frequency. Moats and Hidden Worries: What are the risks of this "money-printing machine"? Charging fees means users need to pay additional costs, so how can Polymarket sustain this? The platform boasts a clear triple moat: First, it possesses unparalleled liquidity depth in the prediction market sector, crucial for high-volume traders; second, compared to traditional gambling's 5%-10% commission and Kalshi's 1%-3.5%, its 0.45% peak fee rate still offers an overwhelming cost advantage; third, ICE's entry not only brings capital but also data distribution capabilities. ICE plans to integrate Polymarket's real-time prediction data with global institutional clients, creating a "second growth curve" beyond transaction fees. However, risks cannot be ignored: Short-term trading volume fluctuations: Polymarket's monthly trading volume peaked at $1.026 billion in November 2025 before falling to $543 million in December. Will fees exacerbate this trend? However, considering the positive effects of increased market depth and narrower spreads after the introduction of Maker Rebate, long-term trading volume is expected to rise. Competitive Landscape: Kalshi holds a first-mover advantage in the US compliant market (approximately $260 million in revenue by 2025), Hyperliquid is attempting to enter the prediction market arena through "Outcome Trading" (FDV approximately $16 billion), and Predict.fun attracts users with DeFi yields. Regulatory Uncertainty: Despite obtaining a No-Action Letter from the CFTC and acquiring the compliant exchange QCX, changes in the US regulatory environment remain a Damocles' sword hanging over the prediction market. Postscript From free to paid, from the crypto market to global sporting events, Polymarket is undergoing a meticulously planned business model upgrade. While it can generate millions weekly from the crypto market alone, the sports market—a behemoth accounting for nearly 40% of the platform's trading volume and boasting 30 times the liquidity of the crypto market—is only just beginning to charge fees. Polymarket's story offers a thought-provoking model: the true value of a platform may not lie in how much money it earns at any given moment, but in proving its confidence to "charge when it wants." When the pie is big enough and the moat is deep enough, opening the floodgates to fees is only a matter of time. And this "money-printing machine," already in preparation, only had its start button pressed on February 18th.
